rejection.reason: The requested hand object is the second-order noncommutativity term for two finite-state continuous-time Markov generators, not an unresolved causal-identification gap. For constant matrices A and B, the Taylor/Baker-Campbell-Hausdorff expansion gives exp(t(A+B)) - exp(tA)exp(tB) at order t^2 as a commutator term; substituting the illness-death generators yields the advertised alpha*(gamma-beta)/2 death-probability coefficient. Aalen-Johansen/product-integral theory already supplies the Markov multistate generator-to-transition-probability semantics, and the separable-effects illness-death papers supply the causal estimand layer. The remaining proposed certificate would therefore collapse to standard Markov-semigroup algebra plus separable-effect definition unfolding, which the topic explicitly asked Stage -1.1 to reject.

evidence_refs.claim: Establishes product formulas for semigroups of operators; the finite-matrix constant-generator case underlying exp(t(A+B)) versus exp(tA)exp(tB) is routine semigroup algebra.
evidence_refs.author: Trotter
evidence_refs.venue: Proceedings of the American Mathematical Society

evidence_refs.claim: Gives finite-state Markov transition probabilities from intensities via differential equations and product integrals, the standard multistate generator semantics used by the hand object.
evidence_refs.author: Aalen and Johansen

evidence_refs.claim: Defines separable direct and indirect effects in a continuous-time illness-death model using transition hazards and identifies risk-scale functionals, leaving no new content in the constant-generator commutator arithmetic itself.
evidence_refs.author: Breum, Munch, Gerds, and Martinussen

evidence_refs.excerpt: collapsed into standard observed-law kernel algebra rather than a new flagship identification object

suggested_directions: Re-run with a non-Markov or partially observed illness-death target: characterize when transition-probability data identify the sign or zero of a disease/death component interaction, rather than computing it from known primitive generators.

Re-run with treatment-affected time-varying common causes in continuous time: ask for an identification formula that preserves a separable component-hazard decomposition beyond the baseline-covariate illness-death setup.

Re-run with a T-time or multi-policy commutator criterion anchored to the banked reviewer request, requiring a decision procedure from primitive kernels rather than a two-state or two-time product expansion.

literature_map:
1. Stensrud et al. (JASA 2022) introduce separable effects for competing events via decomposed treatment components and distinct pathways.
2. Stensrud et al. (Lifetime Data Analysis 2021) generalize separable effects to broader time-varying structures and weaker identification conditions.
3. Martinussen and Stensrud (Biometrics 2023) move separable direct/indirect effects into continuous time and derive influence-function-based estimators.
4. Breum, Munch, Gerds, and Martinussen (Lifetime Data Analysis 2024) specialize separable effects to a continuous-time illness-death model, the closest survival anchor.
5. Aalen and Johansen (Scandinavian Journal of Statistics 1978) give the product-integral/transition-matrix foundation for Markov multi-state generator semantics.
6. Datta and Satten (Statistics & Probability Letters 2001) show Aalen-Johansen stage-occupation probabilities retain validity beyond Markov transition-probability claims.
7. Putter and Spitoni (Statistical Methods in Medical Research 2018), Allignol et al. (Lifetime Data Analysis 2014), and Bakoyannis (Lifetime Data Analysis 2021) study non-Markov or partially non-Markov transition probability estimation.
8. Diaz, Hoffman, and Hejazi (Lifetime Data Analysis 2024) extend longitudinal modified treatment policies to competing-risk survival outcomes.
9. Diaz and van der Laan (Biometrics 2012) provide the stochastic-intervention substrate behind later policy-effect work.
10. Roysland, Ryalen, Nygard, and Didelez (JRSSB 2025) provide continuous-time graphical criteria for event-history intervention identification.
11. The live literature gaps are about time-varying confounding, non-Markov observability, and policy/intensity decomposition; the submitted constant-generator commutator coefficient is routine semigroup/BCH arithmetic.
12. The closest possible open direction would require withholding the primitive generators or weakening Markov/observation assumptions, so the commutator becomes an identified object rather than an already-computed algebraic term.
prior_proposal_map:
No active proposal exists yet for eid_survival_generator_commutator_v1; only the new pipeline/state artifacts are present under research/eid_survival_generator_commutator.
Primary mined comparator: _bank/failed/eid_policy_commutator_phase_v1, including README, three rejected proposal angles, and reviewer JSONs angle0_v1, angle1_v1, angle1_v2, angle2_v1.
The policy-commutator bank failed because the object kept collapsing to routine extended g-formula algebra, a definitional product-zero phase, or an underanchored representation-quotient obstruction.
Highest-yield reviewer gaps ask for explicit ordered kernels, residual terms in commutator decompositions, and a genuinely T-time or multi-policy commutator criterion.
Secondary mined comparator: _bank/failed/eid_compositional_reallocation_effect_v1, useful mainly as a negative template where elementary constrained-gradient/support-span iff results were not enough without inference, support-boundary, or estimator theory.
Cross-track linking found the submitted survival generator object closest to the policy-commutator failure mode: a promising commutator phrase whose supplied hand object reduces to direct algebra unless the target is broadened.
